name: Amortised Analysis
def: A technique for bounding the average cost per operation over a worst-case sequence, so occasional expensive steps are charged against many cheap ones.
problem: Characterise data structures whose individual operations spike but whose long-run cost is low (e.g. dynamic-array growth).
mechanism: Three lenses: aggregate (total cost / n), accounting (prepay credits on cheap ops to fund expensive ones), and potential (define Φ over the structure’s state; amortised cost = actual + ΔΦ).
benefits: Tighter, honest bounds than per-op worst case
Explains why doubling / path compression are efficient
limits: Says nothing about a single operation’s latency
Not worst-case-per-op — bad for hard real-time
Choosing a good potential function is an art
failures: Quoting amortised O(1) where tail latency matters (real-time / interactive)
Mixing amortised and worst-case bounds in one argument
whenNot: Per-operation latency guarantees are required (use worst-case bounds)
myths: "Amortised = average-case" — amortised is worst-case over a sequence, with no probability assumptions
